questions.questionText: The time taken by a block of mass $m$ to slide down from the highest point to the lowest point on a rough inclined plane is $50 \%$ more compared to the time taken by the same block on identical inclined smooth plane. Both inclined planes are at $45^{\circ}$ with the horizontal. The coefficient of kinetic friction between the rough inclined surface and block is $\_\_\_\_$
questions.subject: Physics
questions.solution:
For smooth inclined plane :

When the plane is smooth, there is no friction. The only forces acting on the block along the incline are the component of gravity ( $\mathrm{mg} \sin \theta$ ).

According to Newton's Second Law :

$$ \mathrm{F}_{\mathrm{net}}=\mathrm{ma} $$

$$\Rightarrow $$ $\mathrm{mg} \sin \theta=\mathrm{ma}_{\mathrm{s}}$

$$\Rightarrow $$ $$ \mathrm{a}_{\mathrm{s}}=\mathrm{g} \sin \theta $$

Using the second equation of motion, $S=u t+\frac{1}{2} a t^2$, where the block starts from rest $(u=0)$ and travels a distance L (length of the incline):

$$ \mathrm{L}=\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{a}_{\mathrm{s}} \mathrm{t}_{\mathrm{s}}^2 $$

$$\Rightarrow $$ $$ t_s=\sqrt{\frac{2 L}{a_s}}=\sqrt{\frac{2 L}{g \sin \theta}} $$

For rough inclined plane :

On a rough plane, kinetic friction ( $\mathrm{f}_{\mathrm{k}}$ ) acts upwards against the motion.

The normal force is $\mathrm{N}=\mathrm{mg} \cos \theta$, so $\mathrm{f}_{\mathrm{k}}=\mu_{\mathrm{k}} \mathrm{N}=\mu_{\mathrm{k}} \mathrm{mg} \cos \theta$.

The net force along the incline is :

$$ \mathrm{F}_{\mathrm{net}}=\mathrm{mg} \sin \theta-\mu_{\mathrm{k}} \mathrm{mg} \cos \theta $$

$$\Rightarrow $$ $\mathrm{ma}_{\mathrm{r}}=\mathrm{m}\left(\mathrm{g} \sin \theta-\mu_{\mathrm{k}} \mathrm{g} \cos \theta\right)$

$$\Rightarrow $$ $$ \mathrm{a}_{\mathrm{r}}=\mathrm{g}\left(\sin \theta-\mu_{\mathrm{k}} \cos \theta\right) $$

The time taken $\mathrm{t}_{\mathrm{r}}$ to travel the same distance L is:

$$ t_r=\sqrt{\frac{2 L}{a_r}}=\sqrt{\frac{2 L}{g\left(\sin \theta-\mu_k \cos \theta\right)}} $$

It is given that $\mathrm{t}_{\mathrm{r}}$ is $50 \%$ more than $\mathrm{t}_{\mathrm{s}}$.

$$ \mathrm{t}_{\mathrm{r}}=\mathrm{t}_{\mathrm{s}}+0.5 \mathrm{t}_{\mathrm{s}}=1.5 \mathrm{t}_{\mathrm{s}}=\frac{3}{2} \mathrm{t}_{\mathrm{s}} $$

$$\Rightarrow $$ $$ \mathrm{t}_{\mathrm{r}}^2=\frac{9}{4} \mathrm{t}_{\mathrm{s}}^2 $$

Substituting the expressions for $\mathrm{t}_{\mathrm{r}}^2$ and $\mathrm{t}_{\mathrm{s}}^2$ :

$$ \frac{2 L}{g\left(\sin \theta-\mu_k \cos \theta\right)}=\frac{9}{4}\left(\frac{2 L}{g \sin \theta}\right) $$

$$\Rightarrow $$ $\frac{1}{\sin \theta-\mu_k \cos \theta}=\frac{9}{4 \sin \theta}$

$$\Rightarrow $$ $4 \sin \theta=9 \sin \theta-9 \mu_{\mathrm{k}} \cos \theta$

$$\Rightarrow $$ $9 \mu_{\mathrm{k}} \cos \theta=5 \sin \theta$

$$\Rightarrow $$ $$ \mu_{\mathrm{k}}=\frac{5}{9} \tan \theta $$

The angle of inclination is $\theta=45^{\circ}$

$$ \mu_{\mathrm{k}}=\frac{5}{9} \tan 45^{\circ}=\frac{5}{9} $$

Therefore, the correct option is (C).

questions.questionText: A light spring balance hangs from the hook of the other light spring balance and a block of mass $$M$$ $$kg$$ hangs from the former one. Then the true statement about the scale reading is
options: The scale of the lower one reads $$M$$ $$kg$$ and of the upper one zero
The reading of the two scales can be anything but the sum of the reading will be $$M$$ $$kg$$
questions.subject: Physics
questions.solution:
Question says, both spring balance are light that means springs are massless.
The Earth pulls the block by a force $$Mg.$$ The block in turn exerts a force $$Mg$$ on the spring of spring balance $${S_1}$$ which therefore shows a reading of $$Mkgf.$$

The spring $${S_1}$$ is massless. Therefore it exerts a force of $$Mg$$ on the spring of spring balance $${S_2}$$ which shows the reading of $$Mkgf.$$

questions.questionText: A boy pushes a box of mass 2 kg with a force $$\overrightarrow F  = \left( {20\widehat i + 10\widehat j} \right)N$$ on a frictionless surface. If the box was initially at rest, then ___________ m is displacement along the x-axis after 10s.
questions.subject: Physics
questions.solution:
$$\overrightarrow F = 20\widehat i + 10\widehat j$$

$$\overrightarrow a = {{\overrightarrow F } \over m} = {{20\widehat i + 10\widehat j} \over 2} = 10\widehat i + 5\widehat j$$

$$ \therefore $$ $$\overrightarrow s = {1 \over 2}\overrightarrow a {t^2} = {1 \over 2}\left( {10\widehat i + 5\widehat j} \right) \times {\left( {10} \right)^2}$$

$$ = 50\left( {10\widehat i + 5\widehat j} \right)m$$

$$ \therefore $$ Displacement along x-axis

= 50 $$\times$$ 10 = 500 m

questions.questionText: For a free body diagram shown in the figure, the four forces are applied in the 'x' and 'y' directions. What additional force must be applied and at what angle with positive x-axis so that the net acceleration of body is zero?
questions.subject: Physics
questions.solution:
Resultant of already applied forces $$ = - \widehat i - \widehat j$$

$$\Rightarrow$$ Force required to balance $$ = \widehat i + \widehat j$$

$$\Rightarrow$$ Force required $$ = \sqrt 2 $$ N in magnitude at angle 45$$^\circ$$ with +ve x-axis

questions.questionText: A wooden block, initially at rest on the ground, is pushed by a force which increases linearly with time $$t$$. Which of the following curve best describes acceleration of the block with time :
questions.subject: Physics
questions.solution:
Acceleration (a) $$=\frac{f-F}{m}$$

When applied force became equal to $$f_{\max }$$, block will start moving.

As $$F$$ increases linearly, so acceleration of also moving block will increase linearly.

questions.questionText: At any instant the velocity of a particle of mass $$500 \mathrm{~g}$$ is $$\left(2 t \hat{i}+3 t^{2} \hat{j}\right) \mathrm{ms}^{-1}$$. If the force acting on the particle at $$t=1 \mathrm{~s}$$ is $$(\hat{i}+x \hat{j}) \mathrm{N}$$. Then the value of $$x$$ will be:
questions.subject: Physics
questions.solution:
Given the velocity vector of a particle $v = (2t \hat{i}+3 t^{2} \hat{j}) \, \text{ms}^{-1}$, the acceleration $a$ is the derivative of the velocity vector with respect to time. So, we have:

$a = \frac{dv}{dt} = (2 \hat{i} + 6t \hat{j}) \, \text{ms}^{-2}$.

At $t=1 \, \text{s}$, the acceleration $a$ is $(2 \hat{i} + 6 \hat{j}) \, \text{ms}^{-2}$.

According to Newton's second law, the force $F$ is equal to the mass $m$ times acceleration $a$. The mass $m$ is given as $500 \, \text{g}$, or equivalently, $0.5 \, \text{kg}$.

Therefore, the force $F$ on the particle at $t=1 \, \text{s}$ is:

$F = m \cdot a = 0.5 \cdot (2 \hat{i} + 6 \hat{j}) = (1 \hat{i} + 3 \hat{j}) \, \text{N}$.

So, the force acting on the particle at $t=1 \, \text{s}$ is $(\hat{i} + x \hat{j}) \, \text{N}$, where $x=3$.

Therefore, the answer is $x=3$.

questions.questionText:
A body of mass 1 kg is suspended with the help of two strings making angles as shown in figure. Magnitudes of tensions $\mathrm{T}_1$ and $\mathrm{T}_2$, respectively, are (in N ) :

(Take acceleration due to gravity $10 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}^2$ )
questions.subject: Physics
questions.solution: $$\begin{aligned} & \mathrm{T}_1=\mathrm{mg} \cos 30^{\circ} \\ & \mathrm{T}_2=\mathrm{mg} \sin 30^{\circ} \end{aligned}$$

questions.questionText: A body of mass $4 \mathrm{~kg}$ experiences two forces $\vec{F}_1=5 \hat{i}+8 \hat{j}+7 \hat{k}$ and $\overrightarrow{\mathrm{F}}_2=3 \hat{i}-4 \hat{j}-3 \hat{k}$. The acceleration acting on the body is :
questions.subject: Physics
questions.solution:
To find the acceleration acting on the body, we first need to determine the resultant force acting on the body by adding the two forces $\vec{F}_1$ and $\vec{F}_2$ vectorially. Then, we apply Newton's second law of motion, which states that the acceleration $\vec{a}$ of a body is directly proportional to the total force $\vec{F}$ acting on it and inversely proportional to the mass $m$ of the body :

$$ \vec{F} = m \cdot \vec{a} $$

or

$$ \vec{a} = \frac{\vec{F}}{m} $$

Let's start by adding the forces:

$$ \vec{F}_1 + \vec{F}_2 = (5 \hat{i}+8 \hat{j}+7 \hat{k}) + (3 \hat{i}-4 \hat{j}-3 \hat{k}) $$

Performing the addition component-wise:

$$ \vec{F}_{\text{total}} = (5 + 3)\hat{i} + (8 - 4)\hat{j} + (7 - 3)\hat{k} \ \vec{F}_{\text{total}} = 8 \hat{i} + 4 \hat{j} + 4 \hat{k} $$

Now, let's use the formula for acceleration with $m = 4 \mathrm{~kg}$:

$$ \vec{a} = \frac{\vec{F}_{\text{total}}}{m} = \frac{8 \hat{i} + 4 \hat{j} + 4 \hat{k}}{4 \mathrm{~kg}} $$

Divide each component by the mass:

$$ \vec{a} = 2 \hat{i} + 1 \hat{j} + 1 \hat{k} $$

So, the acceleration acting on the body is:

$$ \vec{a} = 2 \hat{i} + \hat{j} + \hat{k}$$

Thus, the correct option is:

Option A :

$$2 \hat{i}+\hat{j}+\hat{k}$$

questions.questionText: A cricket player catches a ball of mass $120 \mathrm{~g}$ moving with $25 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}$ speed. If the catching process is completed in $0.1 \mathrm{~s}$ then the magnitude of force exerted by the ball on the hand of player will be (in SI unit) :
questions.subject: Physics
questions.solution:
The first step in solving this problem is to calculate the change in momentum of the ball when it is caught. The change in momentum, or impulse, is the product of the mass of the ball and the change in velocity (as momentum is mass times velocity).

The ball is initially moving with a velocity of $v_i = 25 \mathrm{~m/s}$ before the catch and finally comes to rest with a velocity of $v_f = 0 \mathrm{~m/s}$ after the catch. Since the ball is caught, the final velocity is zero. The change in velocity $$\Delta v = v_f - v_i = 0 - 25 = -25 \mathrm{~m/s}.$$ Remember that the direction of the force exerted by the ball on the hand will be opposite to the direction of the ball's initial motion.

The mass of the ball $m$ is given as $120 \mathrm{~g}$ which needs to be converted into kilograms to maintain SI units: $$m = 120 \mathrm{~g} = 120 \times 10^{-3} \mathrm{~kg} = 0.12 \mathrm{~kg}.$$

Now we can calculate the change in momentum (impulse): $$\Delta p = m \Delta v = 0.12 \mathrm{~kg} \times (-25 \mathrm{~m/s}).$$

Substituting the values we get: $$\Delta p = 0.12 \times -25 = -3 \mathrm{~kg \cdot m/s}.$$

The negative sign indicates that the change in momentum is in the opposite direction of the ball's initial motion, which makes sense because the ball's velocity is reduced to zero.

The magnitude of the impulse is independent of the sign and is $3 \mathrm{~kg \cdot m/s}$.

Impulse is also equal to the average force exerted on the ball times the time interval during which the force is exerted. We can use the formula: $$\Delta p = F_{avg} \Delta t$$

Where $F_{avg}$ is the average force and $\Delta t$ is the time interval of $0.1 \mathrm{~s}$. Re-arranging the formula to solve for $F_{avg}$ gives us: $$F_{avg} = \frac{\Delta p}{\Delta t}.$$

Substituting the known values we have: $$F_{avg} = \frac{3}{0.1} = 30 \mathrm{~N}.$$

The magnitude of the average force exerted by the hand of the player to catch the ball is $30 \mathrm{~N}$.

questions.questionText: A block is kept on a frictionless inclined surface with angle of inclination $$'\,\alpha \,'.$$ The incline is given an acceleration $$a$$ to keep the block stationary. Then $$a$$ is equal to
questions.subject: Physics
questions.solution:
Acceleration of the block is to the right. Pseudo force acting on the block to the left.


From diagram we can say,

m$$a$$cos$$\alpha $$ = m$$g$$sin$$\alpha $$

$$ \Rightarrow a = g\tan \alpha $$
